metadata.title: Spec vs. Prompt: Same Claude, Different Login
metadata.description_blurb: 'Write me a login function' gets MD5 hashes and a global dict. The same Claude 20 minutes later, with a spec, gets bcrypt, parameterized queries, and a clarifying question — the difference is entirely in what the human does first.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Same Claude. Same model. Prompt one, four words: 'write me a login function.' Prompt two, eight sentences, written after ten minutes of spec work. The second prompt gets bcrypt, parameterized queries, and a clarifying question. The first gets MD5 hashes that have been cryptographically broken since 2010. The difference is entirely in what the human does before they type. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, 'write me a login function'

beats.narration_text: Claude returns a function using hashlib.md5 and a Python dict called USERS. MD5 is cryptographically broken for password storage — has been since roughly 2010. The USERS dict does not persist across restarts and accepts an empty string as a valid password. Seth almost committed this. He did not because he spotted it in review. Most students do not spot it.
beats.new_visual_element: MD5 + global USERS dict — two security failures in first output

beats.narration_text: Four minutes of Gru work before the second prompt. Read three existing files to understand the project's auth expectations. Name the boundary — what the login function is responsible for and what it is not. Write the handoff condition — what does a passing login function look like in a test.
beats.new_visual_element: Gru spec work — read files / name boundary / write handoff condition

beats.narration_text: The second prompt produces bcrypt, parameterized queries, and then — a clarifying question. 'Should the session token be stored in a cookie or returned in the response body?' Claude did not ask this in prompt one. The spec made the question visible. The question is better than guessing — and it only appears when the spec existed to make the ambiguity apparent.
beats.new_visual_element: Claude's clarifying question — spec unlocked the ask

beats.narration_text: Same model. Same weights. Same Claude. The spec is what changed the artifact — not the model, not a different temperature, not a magic prompt pattern. The spec is what the human wrote before asking. That is the whole lesson.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — same model / different artifact / spec is the difference

beats.narration_text: Write the spec before the prompt. Paste this into Claude — it will ask the spec questions before generating anything.

props.command: Before writing any code, ask me: (1) what does this function receive and return, (2) what is the security context — authentication, storage, session management, (3) what framework and libraries are already in use, (4) what does a passing test look like. Then write the function.

beats.narration_text: If this saves you from shipping a broken login function, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
